revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:چیگیو]][[ja:知行]][[zh:知行]]
{{Refimprove|date=September 2019|qq=}} '''Chigyō''' (知行) refers to the concept of territorial governance exercised by landowners in medieval and early modern {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The term "Chigyō" began to be used during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} and evolved over different eras. Unlike European {{ill|Serfdom|en|Serfdom|qid=Q103350|lt=Serfdom|1=Serfdom}}-based lords who owned land and people as private property, Japanese landowners held rights and responsibilities within a structured system of taxation and governance. Understanding Chigyō is crucial for grasping the role of the ruling class in Japanese history. == Historical Development == === The Emergence of the Chigyō Concept === In the 9th and 10th centuries, Japan's governance system transitioned from the centralized [[Ritsuryō system]] to a more decentralized structure known as the [[Myōden system]], relying on wealthy farmers called {{ill|Tato|en|Tato|qid=Q450377|lt=Tato|1=Tato}}. By the 11th century, land organization into [[Shōen]] estates and public domains (later reorganized into {{ill|Gun|en|Gun|qid=Q110155210|lt=Gun|1=Gun}}, [[:en:Gō|Gō]], and {{ill|Ho|en|Ho|qid=Q224477|lt=Ho|1=Ho}}) became the basis for taxation under the [[Shōen Kōryōsei]] system. This gave rise to landowners (ryōshu), who managed these estates and collected rents, taxes, and labor from the land and its people. Their governance rights were termed "Chigyō," "Ryōchi,"<ref>The term "Ryōchi" commonly used today to mean "territory" originally referred to the governance of land and people, encompassing broader control than mere land ownership.</ref> "Ryōshō," or "Shinshi." These rights varied based on the landowner’s status and Rank. For instance: - Local [[Myōden]] were controlled by [[Nanushi]] (village leaders) of the [[Hyakushō]] class. - Estates and larger districts were managed by {{ill|Xiaohan|en|Xiaohan|qid=Q1190989|lt=Xiaohan|1=Xiaohan}}, [[Gunji]], [[Gōji]], and [[:en:Hoji|Hoji]], often drawn from the {{ill|Bushi|en|Bushi|qid=Q1209458|lt=Bushi|1=Bushi}} class, evolving into the {{ill|Jitō|en|Jitō|qid=Q1329805|lt=Jitō|1=Jitō}} of the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}. - Prominent nobles, temples, and institutions like the [[Sekkan-ke]] and [[Kuge]] held vast domains, sometimes controlling entire provinces as [[Chigyōkoku]] (territories granted to specific officials for tax collection). The Chigyōkoku system proliferated in the late Heian period as noble families and religious institutions extended their tax rights over entire provinces. Medieval documents indicate that terms like Chigyō, Ryōchi, Ryōshō, and Shinshi were used interchangeably to denote land control. However, some scholars argue that while "Shinshi" referred to land control, "Chigyō" primarily referred to the right to distribute land revenues. === Transformation of the Chigyō Concept === During the late Kamakura period, {{ill|Jitō|en|Jitō|qid=Q1329805|lt=Jitō|1=Jitō}} sought to consolidate fragmented governance structures into unified systems called [[Ichien Chigyō]]. This trend intensified during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} under the [[Shugo Daimyō]]'s [[Shugo Ruling System]] and culminated in the [[Daimyō Ruling System]] of the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|qid=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}. By this time, Chigyō specifically referred to land granted or guaranteed by a superior lord to a vassal, becoming the basis for military service and administration. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, Chigyō holdings were measured by {{ill|Koku|en|Koku|qid=Q855048|lt=Koku|1=Koku}} and were a key determinant of a samurai’s obligations and social Rank. Some regions also employed [[Eiryō]] and [[Han'eiryō]] methods to calculate Chigyō. === Decline and Abolition === With the [[Meiji Restoration]], samurai land and revenue rights were abolished, and the concept of Chigyō disappeared as Japan transitioned to a modern, centralized state under the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|en|Meiji Constitution|qid=Q122804|lt=Meiji Constitution|1=Meiji Constitution}}.
== See Also == * [[Chigyōkoku]] * [[Ichien Chigyō]] * [[Tōchigyō]] * {{ill|Lehen|en|Lehen|qid=Q323073|lt=Lehen|1=Lehen}} * [[Aikyū]] * [[Bunchi]] * [[Chiho Chigyō]] * [[Kuramai-tori]] == External Links == * {{Kotobank|qq=}}
{{Japanese-history-stub|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|知行|version=89773033|comment=Imported full ja history}}
